intitule: Reading: The Benefits of Volunteering - Comprehension and Analysis

enonce.texte:
Read the text below carefully and answer the questions that follow.

Why Volunteer? More Than Just a Good Deed

Every day, millions of people around the world dedicate their time and energy to helping others without expecting any financial reward. These individuals are known as volunteers. While the primary goal of volunteering is to benefit the community, research shows that the volunteers themselves gain a great deal from the experience. For many young people, especially those in their final years of secondary school, volunteering is a powerful way to build a better future for themselves and their society.

Firstly, volunteering is an excellent way to develop important life skills. When you volunteer at a local library, help organize a community sports event, or assist in a hospital, you are not just helping others; you are also learning to communicate more effectively, work in a team, and solve problems creatively. For instance, a student who volunteers to teach computer skills to the elderly will inevitably improve their own patience and public speaking abilities. These skills are highly valued by universities and employers.

Furthermore, volunteering has a significant positive impact on a person's self-esteem and mental well-being. Helping those who are less fortunate often provides a new perspective on one's own challenges. Knowing that your actions have made a tangible difference in someone's life, such as helping a child learn to read or providing a warm meal to a homeless person, brings a deep sense of satisfaction and purpose. This feeling of being useful can reduce stress and combat feelings of loneliness or isolation.

On a larger scale, community service is the backbone of a strong and healthy society. When citizens take an active role in solving local problems, whether it's cleaning up a polluted river or tutoring students in a struggling school, they create a more connected and resilient community. Volunteers fill critical gaps in services that governments and private companies may not be able to address alone. In a country like Cameroon, where community spirit is already strong, volunteering reinforces the values of solidarity and mutual support, paving the way for a more prosperous and peaceful future for everyone.

In conclusion, while the primary beneficiaries of volunteering are those who receive help, the rewards for the volunteer are just as profound. From acquiring practical skills for the job market to building a meaningful and purposeful life, the act of giving your time is an investment in your own future as much as it is a gift to your community.

questions.texte: According to the text, what are the two main categories of benefits that volunteers gain from their experience?
questions.reponse: The two main categories of benefits are: 1) the development of important life skills (e.g., communication, teamwork, problem-solving), and 2) a positive impact on self-esteem and mental well-being (e.g., satisfaction, purpose, reduced stress).

questions.texte: Identify and list three specific life skills mentioned in paragraph 2 that a student can develop through volunteering.
questions.reponse: Three specific life skills mentioned are: 1) communicating more effectively, 2) working in a team, and 3) solving problems creatively. (Patience and public speaking are also acceptable).

questions.texte: Analyze the author's claim that volunteering helps build a 'more connected and resilient community' (paragraph 4). Based on the text, explain two mechanisms through which this happens.
questions.reponse: The author claims volunteering builds a more connected and resilient community through two main mechanisms: 1) When citizens take an active role in solving local problems (e.g., cleaning a river, tutoring in a school), they create a more connected community by working together towards a common goal. 2) Volunteers fill critical gaps in services that governments and private companies cannot address alone, making the community more resilient (able to withstand and recover from difficulties) because it is not solely dependent on official institutions for every need.

questions.texte: Distinguish between the individual benefits of volunteering and the collective benefits for society as described in the text. Provide one example of each from the reading.
questions.reponse: Individual benefits are personal gains for the volunteer, such as improved self-esteem and a sense of satisfaction (e.g., 'helping a child learn to read brings a deep sense of satisfaction'). Collective benefits are gains for the whole community, such as a stronger, healthier society (e.g., 'volunteers fill critical gaps in services that governments... may not be able to address alone').

questions.texte: Justify the author's final statement: 'the act of giving your time is an investment in your own future as much as it is a gift to your community.' Use evidence from the text to support your justification.
questions.reponse: The author's statement is justified because the text provides clear evidence that volunteering offers dual, equally valuable returns. As a 'gift to your community', the text states that volunteers fill critical service gaps and reinforce values of solidarity (paragraph 4). As an 'investment in your own future', the text explains that volunteering helps develop life skills that are 'highly valued by universities and employers' (paragraph 2) and improves mental well-being, reducing stress and combating loneliness (paragraph 3). Therefore, the volunteer receives tangible personal benefits (skills, well-being, a better future) that are just as significant as the benefits provided to the community, making the act a true investment with personal dividends.

intitule: Grammar: The Infinitive of Purpose - Controlled Practice

enonce.texte:
In English, we can use the infinitive of purpose (to + base verb) to answer the question 'Why?' or express the intention behind an action. For example: She studies hard to pass her exams. (Why does she study hard? To pass her exams.)

Exercise 1: Rewrite the following sentences using the infinitive of purpose. Follow the example.

Example: He went to the town hall. He wanted to register as a volunteer.
Answer: He went to the town hall to register as a volunteer.

questions.texte: She is saving money. She wants to buy school supplies for needy children.
questions.reponse: She is saving money to buy school supplies for needy children.

questions.texte: Many young people join community service clubs. They want to help the less fortunate.
questions.reponse: Many young people join community service clubs to help the less fortunate.

questions.texte: The organization 'Save the Children' is raising funds. It wants to build a new school in a rural area.
questions.reponse: The organization 'Save the Children' is raising funds to build a new school in a rural area.

questions.texte: The volunteers worked all night. They wanted to distribute food to the flood victims before dawn.
questions.reponse: The volunteers worked all night to distribute food to the flood victims before dawn.

questions.texte: He called the NGO's office. He wanted to ask for information about the upcoming environmental campaign.
questions.reponse: He called the NGO's office to ask for information about the upcoming environmental campaign.

intitule: Speaking: Role-Play a Volunteer Recruitment Interview

enonce.texte:
Situation: The local NGO 'Youth for Change' is recruiting volunteers for two upcoming projects: a 'Literacy for All' program (teaching basic reading and writing to adults in a rural community) and a 'Green City' initiative (organizing recycling and urban gardening activities). You will participate in a mock job interview to become a volunteer.

Instructions:

  1. In pairs (Student A and Student B), prepare for the role-play.
  2. Student A (Candidate): Choose one of the two projects. Prepare to explain why you want to join that specific project, what skills you have (e.g., patience, organization, communication), and what you hope to learn from the experience. Use the infinitive of purpose (e.g., 'I want to join this project to help illiterate adults gain confidence.')
  3. Student B (Recruiter): Prepare 3-4 questions to ask the candidate. Your goal is to determine if the candidate is motivated, reliable, and a good fit for the project. Ask about their motivation ('Why do you want to work with us?'), their skills ('What can you bring to this team?'), and their availability.
  4. Perform the 2-3 minute interview in front of the class or with your partner.

Useful Language for the Candidate:

  • 'I'm very interested in this project because...'
  • 'I believe I am a suitable candidate because...'
  • 'I have experience in...' / 'I am a hard-working and motivated person.'
  • 'I want to volunteer to develop my skills in...'

Useful Language for the Recruiter:

  • 'Could you tell me why you want to join this project?'
  • 'What makes you the right person for this role?'
  • 'How would you handle a difficult situation, like a learner who is not progressing?'
  • 'How much time can you commit to this project?'

questions.texte: This is a performance-based task. The teacher will evaluate the candidate's role-play based on the following criteria: 1) Clear expression of motivation using appropriate language (e.g., 'to + verb'), 2) Relevance of personal skills to the chosen project, 3) Fluency and pronunciation during the conversation, and 4) Use of persuasive language and responses to the recruiter's questions.
questions.reponse: There is no single written answer. Evaluation is based on the teacher's observation of the oral performance against the stated criteria. A successful candidate will logically explain their motivation (e.g., 'I want to join the Green City project to make my neighborhood cleaner'), connect their skills to the project's needs, and answer questions thoughtfully.

intitule: Writing: Write a Speech for a Volunteer Award Ceremony

enonce.texte:
Context: Your school is organizing a 'Community Heroes' award ceremony to recognize students who have made an outstanding contribution through volunteering. You have been chosen as one of the award winners for your work with a local charity (e.g., helping at an orphanage, tutoring younger students, organizing a food drive). You must write a 150-200 word speech to deliver at the ceremony.

Instructions: Write your speech. In your speech, you should:

  • Express gratitude to the organizers and those who supported you.
  • Explain the purpose of your volunteer project and the actions you took. Use the infinitive of purpose (e.g., 'I organized a book drive to provide resources for the school library.').
  • Share the impact of your work on the community and on yourself (e.g., skills learned, personal growth).
  • Encourage other students to become volunteers.

Example structure:

Good morning everyone, teachers, fellow students, and honored guests. I am deeply honored to receive this 'Community Hero' award today...

I started volunteering with [name of project] because I wanted [explain your purpose]. Every Saturday, I would [describe your main actions]...

Thanks to this experience, I have learned [mention a skill or value]. More importantly, I have seen how [describe the positive change in the community]...

I urge each of you to find a cause you care about and give your time. Together, we can build a stronger and more compassionate Cameroon. Thank you.

questions.texte: Write your speech. Ensure you follow all the instructions regarding content (gratitude, purpose, impact, encouragement) and use the infinitive of purpose at least twice.
questions.reponse:
(Example of a student's expected answer - content will vary but must meet criteria)

Good morning everyone. I am truly honored to receive this Community Hero award.

I started volunteering with the 'Sunshine Orphanage' to bring joy and educational support to children without families. Every Saturday, I would help them with their homework and organize fun learning games. I also created a small reading corner to encourage a love for books.

This experience has taught me patience and the true meaning of gratitude. Seeing a child's smile when they finally understand a math problem is priceless. I have learned that even small actions can create big changes.

I urge every student here to find a cause you are passionate about. Don't wait for a perfect moment. Volunteer to make a difference, and you will discover that you are also changing yourself. Thank you.
